Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 137 actions; a strong score needs about 78 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
OK, so SPACE from position 4 just resets the player to the start position. It doesn't teleport to the target.
Let me think about this differently. The player is at the start position. The game over happened when the player moved to rows 20-24, cols 14-18.
Looking at the maze layout, the white corridor is at rows 14-38, cols 20-38. The player can move through the white corridor, but can't reach the target.
Wait, maybe the player needs to follow the red bar to reach the target. The red bar is on the right side of the maze, and it has a horizontal segment at the bottom.
Looking at the red boundary:
- [[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18], [42, 14], [41, 15], [40, 14], [39, 15], [38, 14], [38, 18], [39, 18], [40, 19], [40, 39], [39, 40], [12, 40], [11, 39]]
This forms a "C" shape that opens to the left. The interior of the "C" is the white corridor.
Wait, but the red bar is not a death zone. The death zone is at rows 20-24, cols 14-18, which is outside the red bar's bounds.
Let me re-examine the death zone. The player moved from rows 14-18, cols 14-18 to rows 20-24, cols 14-18, and the game ended. So rows 20-24, cols 14-18 is a death zone.
Looking at the image, rows 20-24, cols 14-18 is below the start position. The start position is at rows 8-12, cols 14-18. The player moved DOWN twice to reach rows 20-24, cols 14-18.
Wait, but the player moved from rows 14-18, cols 14-18 to rows 20-24, cols 14-18. So the player moved DOWN once from rows 14-18, cols 14-18.
Looking at the image, rows 14-18, cols 14-18 is below the start position. The player moved DOWN from the start position to rows 14-18, cols 14-18, and then DOWN again to rows 20-24, cols 14-18.
So the death zone is at rows 20-24, cols 14-18, which is two DOWN moves from the start position.
But wait, looking at the image, the start position is at rows 8-12, cols 14-18. The player can move DOWN to rows 14-18, cols 14-18, and then DOWN to rows 20-24, cols 14-18.
But rows 20-24, cols 14-18 is a death zone. So the player can only move DOWN once from the start position.
Let me think about the correct path. The player needs to reach the target at rows 49-55, cols 43-49.
Looking at the image, the target is at the bottom-right of the maze. The red bar is on the right side of the maze. Maybe the player needs to follow the red bar to reach the target.
But the player can't move RIGHT from rows 20-24, cols 26-30. So the player can't reach the red bar.
Wait, maybe the player needs to move to position 4 (cols 38-42) to change the red bar, and then the red bar will extend to the target, allowing the player to move DOWN from rows 20-24, cols 26-30.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the white corridor's segmentation. The white corridor is at rows 14-38, cols 20-38.
Actually, let me look at the black region's segmentation to understand the maze layout. The black region is the wall, and the player moves through the non-black regions.
The black region has 879 pixels and is at rows 7-56, cols 13-50. This is the maze structure.
The white corridor is at rows 14-38, cols 20-38. The player needs to navigate through this white corridor to reach the target.
But the player is stuck in the white corridor. The player can't move RIGHT or DOWN from rows 20-24, cols 26-30.
Wait, maybe the player needs to move to a different position in the white corridor to unlock the path to the target.
Let me try moving the player to rows 14-18, cols 32-36 (position 3) and see if the player can move DOWN from there.
Actually, I already tried this earlier, and the player can't move DOWN from rows 14-18, cols 32-36.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the player needs to use SPACE to activate something when the player is at a specific position.
I already tried SPACE from the start position and position 4, and it just resets the player to the start position.
Maybe SPACE does something different when the player is at rows 20-24, cols 26-30. Let me try this.
First, move to rows 20-24, cols 26-30, and then try SPACE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: SPACE just resets to start. Let me try SPACE from rows 20-24, cols 26-30 (deep in white corridor).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at start. Move to rows 20-24, cols 26-30.
# Path: RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'], intent='solve')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"Player: {n['boundary']}")

# Try SPACE
action(['SPACE'], intent='solve')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"After SPACE: {n['boundary']}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
